The Economic Case for EU Membership: Why Iceland Can't Afford to Stay Outside

By Sigríður Björnsdóttir | Published 20 February 2026

As Iceland prepares for its historic referendum on EU membership, the economic arguments for joining are compelling and increasingly difficult to ignore. While opponents focus on fisheries — admittedly an important sector — they overlook the broader economic transformation that membership would bring.

Food prices in Iceland are among the highest in Europe, with consumers paying 50-70% more than the EU average for basic groceries. EU membership would significantly reduce food prices by eliminating trade barriers and ending the protectionist agricultural policies that keep prices artificially high. A family of four could save hundreds of thousands of krónur annually.

Iceland already follows approximately 70% of EU legislation through the EEA Agreement, but without any say in how those laws are made. We are rule-takers, not rule-makers. Full membership would give Iceland a seat at the table — a voice in the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers — allowing us to shape the regulations we already implement.

The investment case is equally strong. EU membership would bring unprecedented economic stability, making Iceland a more attractive destination for foreign direct investment. The adoption of the euro would eliminate currency risk, which has plagued Icelandic businesses for decades. Remember the 2008 financial crisis — the króna collapsed by over 50%, wiping out savings and making imports unaffordable overnight. Euro membership would prevent such catastrophic currency crises.

Iceland's tourism industry, now our largest foreign exchange earner, would benefit enormously from EU membership. Simplified travel arrangements, integration into EU transport networks, and the stability of the euro would all boost visitor numbers and spending.

The Nordic experience is instructive. Finland and Sweden, both EU members, have thrived economically since joining. Finland's GDP per capita has grown significantly, and its technology sector — led by companies that benefited from EU single market access — has become a global powerhouse.

Young Icelanders increasingly see their future within Europe. Brain drain is already a serious problem, with talented graduates leaving for opportunities in Copenhagen, London, and Berlin. EU membership would make it easier for Icelanders to study and work across Europe while also making Iceland more attractive to European professionals.

Critics raise valid concerns about fisheries, but modern EU accession negotiations would protect Iceland's vital interests. Croatia's accession in 2013 demonstrated that new member states can negotiate robust protections for sensitive sectors. Iceland's negotiating position is strong — the EU wants Iceland as a member, and fisheries protections would be a prerequisite for any deal.

The status quo — EEA membership without full EU membership — is the worst of both worlds. We follow the rules but don't make them. We pay into EU programmes but have no vote. It's time for Iceland to take its rightful seat at the European table.
